title: I Was in the Wrong Place
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Ithaca in July 2011, Dorothy Cotton opens with the pervasive childhood feeling that she was in the wrong place, that her home on North Greenleaf Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, with its outhouse and its hardness, was alien to something in her very being.

title: Losing My Mother
summary: Cotton recalls her mother dying when she was three, leaving her father, a skinny man with a third-grade education raised on a tenant farm, to raise four small girls through the Depression in a segregated town; she digresses to question the very habit of describing people by color, a language she now finds silly.

title: The Strap, and What Was Done to Him
summary: She describes the strap her father used on the girls, the anger it still stirs in her, and her later understanding that his own mother, home late from caring for a white family, had whipped him the same way; that knowledge leaves her feelings toward him a deep mixture of anger and forgiveness.

title: The Airport Was Special
summary: Cotton recounts her father's move to Buffalo and his work at Bethlehem Steel, the finger he lost to an axe in a Goldsboro tobacco factory, and the poignancy of how special an airport was to a man who never flew, once sullen and hurt simply because he had not been brought along to one.

title: A Real Mixture
summary: She remembers her father's pride at managing a Christmas doll, a tea set, and a shoebox of fruit, and his nervousness before white people, and sums up her feeling for him in 2011 as a real mixture of love, anger, and sadness over the deprivation of his life.

title: There's Your Ready Girl
summary: Cotton recalls the anger she still feels at a white boy who rode through her street singing a slur, then turns to her high school English teacher Rosa Gray, who gave her the lead in every school play and, after she recited a poem, called her a ready girl, a phrase that has stayed with her ever since.

title: She Pleaded for the Prom
summary: Realizing only now that Ms. Gray was the surrogate mother she had lost, Cotton describes the teacher coming to the porch to plead with her proud, poor father to let his star pupil attend the junior-senior prom, and her decades-later understanding that he refused because he could not afford to dress her as well as the other children.

title: Nothing But a Racket
summary: Cotton remembers a brief stepmother, the churchgoing Susie Mae her father admitted he never loved, her father's own scorn for the church as nothing but a racket, and her maternal grandfather, the itinerant preacher Reverend Seymour Pellum, whose visits charged the house with an uneasy energy after he and her father, who had run off with her young mother, came to hate each other.

title: Three Jobs at Shaw
summary: Cotton recounts how Ms. Gray, knowing college was out of reach, called Shaw University and arranged her admission with two jobs, in the dining hall and cleaning the teachers' dormitory; she worked her way through with as many as three jobs and was eventually invited to keep house for the president, Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel.

title: Part Daughter, Part Housekeeper
summary: She describes the complicated, often unhappy years in the Daniels' three-story home, treated as part daughter and part servant, made to work even with the flu, her father marveling at the polished artificial flowers; through a Shakespeare classmate, Harris Sims, she was introduced to an older man named George Cotton.

title: He Came With a Ring
summary: Cotton recalls George arriving with a ring to ask Dr. Daniel for her hand, the president warning that she was very ambitious, and the small wedding held in the president's residence where she had scrubbed and served; she followed Daniel from Shaw to Virginia State College in Petersburg, taking six years in all to finish her degree.

title: A Consciousness Evolving
summary: In Petersburg, Cotton traces a growing consciousness of the wrongness of the system to her childhood, then to the church she joined under Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, its pastor, a regional NAACP head and a good preacher whose extracurricular work and easy company drew her in.

title: The Library We Couldn't Use
summary: As Walker's NAACP secretary, Cotton helped organize the campaign against the segregated public library and Woolworth's, training youngsters in the church to carry picket signs and a simple idea of nonviolence; the movement held mass meetings and invited a young Montgomery preacher, Martin Luther King, whom Walker had met at Virginia Union, to come and speak.

title: He Wanted to Meet the Poet
topic: The Three Months That Became Twenty-Three Years
summary: Cotton recalls reciting a poem on the program the night King spoke in Petersburg, and King asking at the parsonage dinner to meet the young woman who had said it; before leaving he asked Walker to come build the new Southern Christian Leadership Conference in Atlanta and to bring his two aides, James Wood and Cotton, so she told her husband she would help for three months, and stayed twenty-three years.

title: A Warring Organization
summary: Cotton describes finding the Southern Christian Leadership Conference still a tiny operation, three or four people in an office, among them Lily Hunter and Ella Baker, when she arrived a wide-eyed young woman who had never traveled, sensing she was on a track to something new she could not yet name.

title: Finding a Home in Atlanta
summary: She recounts her first weeks in Atlanta, staying briefly with Dora McDonald, who became King's secretary, and longer with an elderly landlady, Ms. Mosley, having her purse snatched soon after her husband left, and slowly learning the ropes of the growing organization.

title: A Master's Degree
summary: Cotton notes that she earned a master's degree from Boston University across these years, hungry for something on the horizon, deciding to march in the 1960 commencement after the struggle of fitting the work in, and going to Atlanta only days after receiving it.

title: King Pulled Up a Chair
summary: Working as Walker's administrative assistant, Cotton recalls King pulling a chair to her cluttered desk to ask about her life, then sending her to the Highlander Folk School, and realizing only later she had been interviewed; Highlander was then under siege as a so-called communist school, its name on a roadside billboard beside Eleanor Roosevelt and King.

title: Esau Jenkins and Johns Island
summary: At Highlander Cotton met Septima Clark and learned the story of Esau Jenkins of Johns Island, who, watching a white man kill a Black boy over a dog while the powerless community could not protest, began teaching riders on his bus why registering to vote mattered; Clark, fired from the Charleston schools for the NAACP, called Jenkins a man with a third-grade education and a PhD mind, the germ of the citizenship education program.

title: Inheriting the Citizenship Program
summary: Cotton describes how cursive writing was demanded at registration as a trick to exclude Black voters, how the state confiscated Highlander's property, and how the Marshall Field Foundation, wanting the literacy and citizenship training to continue, moved the funds to SCLC, which named Cotton director of its Citizenship Education Program.

title: The Dorchester Center
summary: With a church facility secured by Andrew Young at the Dorchester Center in McIntosh, Georgia, Cotton, Young, and Septima Clark traveled the South recruiting people from hotbeds of activity into five-day residential workshops; she recalls a self-described junk man, Albert Minnis, teaching her to lead the invitation by promising up front to pay their way.

title: Septima, Andrew, and Me
summary: Cotton describes the three of them traveling like mother, son, and daughter, Septima Clark teaching the cursive writing needed to register, Andrew Young pressing why a single vote matters, and herself laying out the week's sessions, with itinerant preachers among the students who had to learn that dialogue, not preaching, was how people learn.

title: Start a Conversation
summary: Cotton explains her method of starting a conversation rather than lecturing from the front, and tells of a woman who came back day after day, energized and indignant that the city clerk would test the white families' well water but not hers.

title: Why Aren't You the City Clerk
summary: Cotton recalls answering the indignant woman by asking why she did not become the city clerk herself, the moment the room transformed; King would come to close out the workshops with his metaphor that nobody can ride a back that is not bent, the line that became the subtitle of her book, from victim to victory.

title: It May Be the Last Time
summary: Cotton estimates that between five and eight thousand people came through the monthly workshops and carried the training home, among them Fannie Lou Hamer of Ruleville, who became a regular recruiter; she remembers standing with Andrew Young as a busload from the Delta pulled away and singing that this may be the last time, the two of them crying, because some who left would be killed.

part: The Only Woman at the Table
title: The Only Woman at the Table
summary: Cotton reflects that she rarely thought about gender at first, going along with the pattern of the time as the only woman at the executive table, asked to pour the coffee or take the notes until staffer Jack O'Dell insisted she keep her seat; she knew her job was important and that she was smarter than some of the men, and let them be who they thought they needed to be.

part: The Only Woman at the Table
title: A Team of Wild Horses
summary: She describes King as a focused listener presiding over what he fondly called his team of wild horses, the fighting energy Hosea Williams brought to budget arguments, the others like C.T. Vivian and James Bevel debating where to go next, and the day word of James Meredith's march came and King, who waited until he needed to speak, simply said they were going to Mississippi.

title: He Was My Best Friend
summary: Cotton insists a movement is nothing like a formal office, and speaks of being almost always with King, as Andrew Young and Bernard Lee were, running barefoot across a Birmingham street with food for people jailed in a fenced yard; she felt he was her best friend, a closeness she illustrates with an easy evening over gin and tonics.

title: Greens and Pork Chops in Paris
summary: Cotton remembers the 1964 Nobel Prize journey, teasing King that he had come all the way to Paris and wanted collard greens and pork chops from an expatriate's soul-food restaurant, the staff traveling like a family; on the flights to Oslo, King and Coretta rode separate planes for their children's sake, and King's mother, Alberta, rose in the aisle to sing as the sun came up.

title: The First Time He Cried
summary: Cotton recalls how King loved to sing and to slip away to Jamaica or Bermuda to write, and how the first time she ever saw him cry, tears running down his cheeks, was when J. Edgar Hoover publicly called him a notorious liar, a wound she then had to help him bear.

title: Giggly Like a Child
summary: Cotton describes the Atlanta staff meeting where the team debated whether King should speak out against the Vietnam War, and how he left it to come up to her apartment, giggly like a child, and sit in her gold rocking chair simply telling her what was going on.

title: Maybe I Should Take a Sabbatical
summary: She remembers King in her chair another time, weary and pondering whether to take a sabbatical or a university post offered in England, worn by the mounting criticism, including from the young like Stokely Carmichael, who argued at his kitchen table, quoting Frantz Fanon, that nonviolence had outlived its use.

title: The Last Lone Voice
summary: Cotton recounts King's resolve that if he were the last lone voice for nonviolence he would still speak, holding that the end sought is pre-existent in the means used; when a board member, Marion Logan, signed a letter urging him not to denounce the war, the criticism only deepened his commitment to say what he truly felt.

title: The Threats Didn't Control Us
summary: Cotton says the threats came fast and furiously but were never overt enough to make them stop or change plans; it was those around King, not King himself, who grew anxious when a stranger rushed toward him at an airport, and Andrew Young who, sensing the hatred abroad, surrounded him at marches with men who looked like him to take a bullet meant for the leader.

title: Coming Out of Mourning
summary: Cotton reflects on the hard transition after the assassination, a young staffer observing three years on that they were only then coming out of mourning, the loss of their inspiration and best friend leaving a void no one could fill; Ralph Abernathy, the vice president, walked into the presidency while she could barely relate to the change.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: In Over His Head
summary: Cotton offers a candid, self-described reluctant assessment that it was unfair to ask Abernathy to lead King's team of wild horses, a role she felt was over his head, even as she honors him as a caring pastor, recalling him refusing to pass a police blockade unless the choir members behind him were let through too.

title: I Don't Have Anybody to Help Me
summary: Cotton recalls overhearing Abernathy through the office wall say he had no one to help him, and her tears at the truth of it, and how Andrew Young, sensing she needed to move on, arranged for her to become a Head Start director in Jefferson County, Alabama, three years after King's death, in an SCLC that was no longer the one she had known.

title: Racism Is a Sickness
summary: Asked whether King's violent death changed her, Cotton says she still believes in nonviolence as the way, that intensifying the violence would only have brought many more deaths without breaking what she calls American-style apartheid; the deep racism they faced, she holds, is a sickness, and when people are sick you work to heal them, just as she now wishes she could have approached her own father.

title: Tell Me Who You're Going to Shoot
summary: Cotton recalls challenging Stokely Carmichael, who kept urging people to take up the gun, by asking whom exactly he meant to shoot and whether it would move him toward his goal, and his unanswered silence; she argues that without a clear goal one can justify anything, that nonviolence brings her closer to the world she wants, and even reflects, on the killing of Osama bin Laden, that her belief in nonviolence does not require her to condemn that order.

title: An Answer to a Prayer
summary: Cotton describes how two friends, including Anke Wessels of Cornell's Center for Transformative Action, came to her kitchen table with an idea conceived in the shower to build a Dorothy Cotton Institute, an answer to her private worry over what would become of her papers and awards, and to the absence of a civil rights center in the Northeast to match those in Birmingham, Selma, Montgomery, and Memphis.

title: A Certain Spirit
summary: Cotton insists the institute carry the spiritual quality she felt in the movement, in figures from King to Esau Jenkins to Septima Clark, not merely a head trip; above all she wants people to know the movement was more than marching, which is why she highlights the citizenship education program in her nearly finished book, calling its graduates the people who in a real sense made the movement.

title: Three or Four People
summary: Cotton voices her trouble, some of it she is reluctant to record, that three or four people are lifted up as having made the movement when so many did the daily work, that John Lewis is remembered on the Edmund Pettus Bridge while Amelia Boynton, knocked down by a trooper's horse, is forgotten, and that figures like Jesse Jackson, an occasional worker flown in from Chicago, are wrongly cast as King's intimates by a media that wants a controversial face.

title: The Real Story
summary: Cotton closes on her gratitude for the interview and for the chance to set the real story straight, describing the book manuscript spread across her dining table with an editor's help, her hope not for a media blitz but to keep the history from going down as though three or four people made what so many built.
